Routing

Outpost maps every incoming URL to a template file. Understanding the routing algorithm helps you predict which template renders for any given URL and troubleshoot unexpected 404s or wrong-page results.

The two routers

Outpost has two implementations of the same routing logic, each for a different server environment:

EnvironmentFileHow it works
PHP built-in server (dev) front-router.php Passed to php -S as the router script. Called for every request. Returns false for static files to let PHP serve them natively.
Apache / Nginx (production) index.php at document root A .htaccess (or Nginx rewrite rule) forwards all non-static requests to index.php, which runs the same routing logic.

Both routers implement identical logic — the difference is only in how PHP receives the request and how static files are handled.

Routing algorithm — step by step

For every incoming request, the router follows these steps in order. The first matching step wins.

Step 1: Static files

If the request path corresponds to a real file on disk (not a .php file), serve it directly. This covers CSS, JS, images, fonts, and any other static asset. In the dev server router, returning false tells PHP to serve the file natively. In production, this is handled by Apache/Nginx before PHP is invoked.

This step is why you can reference /outpost/themes/my-theme/assets/style.css in your templates — the web server handles that request before Outpost's PHP code ever runs.

Step 2: System paths

Paths beginning with /outpost/ are reserved for Outpost's admin panel, API, and documentation. These are passed through to their respective PHP handlers:

  • /outpost/admin/ → Admin SPA
  • /outpost/api.php → REST API
  • /outpost/docs/ → Developer documentation (this site)
  • /outpost/track.php → Analytics tracking endpoint

Step 3: Sitemap

A request for /sitemap.xml triggers Outpost's automatic sitemap generator. It scans all published pages and collection items and returns a valid XML sitemap. No template file is needed — this is built into the engine.

Step 4: Load active theme

Outpost reads the active_theme key from the settings table. If no theme is active or the theme directory does not exist, routing stops with a 500 error.

Step 5: Legacy PHP theme check

If the active theme's directory contains an index.php file, Outpost treats it as a legacy PHP theme and requires that file, then exits. No further routing logic runs. This exists for backward compatibility only.

Step 6: Liquid theme routing

For Liquid themes (no index.php), the URL path is parsed and matched against templates using the following sub-steps:

Homepage

A request for / maps to index.html in the theme root.

Static page templates

A single-segment path like /about maps to about.html. The router takes the first path segment, strips any leading slash, and looks for a file with that name plus .html in the theme root.

Examples:

URLTemplate
/aboutabout.html
/contactcontact.html
/blogblog.html
/servicesservices.html

Collection URL pattern matching

Before assuming a two-segment URL is a 404, the router checks it against all collection URL patterns. Each collection has a URL pattern configured in Admin → Collections, for example /post/{slug}.

The router compares the incoming URL against each collection's pattern. The {slug} placeholder matches one or more non-slash characters. If a match is found:

  1. The captured slug value is written to $_GET['slug']
  2. The collection's slug is noted (e.g., post)
  3. The router looks for a template named {collection-slug}.html (e.g., post.html)
  4. If that template exists, it is rendered
Collection: "post"
URL pattern: /post/{slug}

Request: /post/hello-world
  → slug = "hello-world" → $_GET['slug'] = 'hello-world'
  → template: post.html

More URL pattern examples:

URL patternExample URLTemplate$_GET['slug']
/post/{slug}/post/hello-worldpost.htmlhello-world
/articles/{slug}/articles/my-articlearticles.htmlmy-article
/portfolio/{slug}/portfolio/project-nameportfolio.htmlproject-name
/products/{slug}/products/widget-proproducts.htmlwidget-pro
/{slug}/hello-world(collection slug).htmlhello-world
Note — Only {slug} is a supported placeholder. It matches one path segment (no slashes). Multi-level patterns like /blog/{year}/{slug} are not supported. Keep URL patterns to two segments: a fixed prefix and {slug}.

404 fallback

If no template file and no collection URL pattern match the request, the router checks for a 404.html in the theme root. If found, it renders that template with an HTTP 404 status code. If 404.html does not exist, Outpost returns a plain 404 text response.

Draft blocking & preview

After a template is selected but before rendering begins, Outpost checks the page's status in the database. Pages with status = 'draft' return a 404 to public visitors. Only published pages render.

Collection item preview

Draft and scheduled collection items can be previewed on the live site using a secure token. From the admin item editor, clicking Preview on an unpublished item generates a one-time token and opens the item's URL with ?preview=TOKEN appended.

The router validates the token against the item's stored preview_token in the database. If valid, the item renders normally — bypassing the status = 'published' filter. Preview pages are never cached and display a "Preview Mode" banner at the bottom of the page.

Tokens are 64-character hex strings, cryptographically random, and regenerated on each preview click. They are only valid for the specific item they were generated for.

Page visibility

After draft checking, the router enforces page visibility settings:

VisibilityBehavior
public All visitors can access. Default for all pages.
members Requires member login. Redirects to login page if not authenticated.
paid Requires paid member status. Redirects to upgrade page if authenticated but not paid.

Visibility is set per-page in the admin editor under the SEO / Visibility section.

Complete routing decision table

RequestMatch conditionResult
/outpost/themes/t/assets/s.cssReal file on diskServed directly (static)
/outpost/admin/Starts with /outpost/Admin panel
/outpost/api.php?action=...Starts with /outpost/API handler
/sitemap.xmlExact matchAuto-generated sitemap XML
/Root pathindex.html
/aboutabout.html exists in themeabout.html
/post/hello-worldMatches collection URL pattern /post/{slug}post.html with slug=hello-world
/unknown-pageNo match404.html (HTTP 404)
Any published page with visibility=membersVisitor not logged inRedirect to login
Any page with status=draftPublic visitorHTTP 404
/post/my-draft?preview=TOKENValid preview tokenRenders draft with preview banner

Writing links in templates

Because Outpost maps clean URLs to template files, links in your HTML must use the route path (the URL visitors see) — never the template filename.

Static page links

<!-- Correct -->
<a href="/">Home</a>
<a href="/about">About</a>
<a href="/contact">Contact</a>

<!-- Wrong — these will 404 -->
<a href="index.html">Home</a>
<a href="about.html">About</a>
<a href="contact.html">Contact</a>

The front-router matches /about and serves about.html internally. A request for /about.html does not match any route and returns a 404.

Collection item links

Inside collection loops, always use {{ item.url }} — Outpost generates the correct URL from the collection's URL pattern automatically:

{% for post in collection.post %}
  <a href="{{ post.url }}">{{ post.title }}</a>
  <!-- outputs: /post/hello-world -->
{% endfor %}

Menu links

Menu loops use {{ item.url }} which stores whatever URL was configured in Admin → Navigation. These are already correct route paths:

{% for item in menu.main %}
  <a href="{{ item.url }}">{{ item.label }}</a>
{% endfor %}
Common mistake when converting static sites — If you are converting an existing HTML website into an Outpost theme, you must update every <a href="page.html"> to <a href="/page">. This is the #1 cause of broken navigation in new themes. The Forge visual tag builder can help with this process.

Defining custom routes

Outpost uses file-based routing. There is no route configuration file, no route registration function, and no engine.php modification needed. Creating a template file IS defining a route.

Static page routes

To add a new route, create an HTML template file in your theme's root directory. The filename (minus .html) becomes the URL:

Create this fileThis URL works
events.html/events
pricing.html/pricing
team.html/team
faq.html/faq

No restart, no rebuild, no registration. The next request to that URL will render the template and auto-scaffold any fields it contains.

Dynamic routes (collections)

For dynamic content with URL parameters (e.g. /event/summer-gala), create a collection in Admin → Collections with a URL pattern like /event/{slug}. The router will automatically match incoming URLs against the pattern and render the corresponding template (event.html).

What you cannot do

Outpost's routing is intentionally simple. These features are not supported:

  • Multi-segment static routes — /blog/categories is not possible as a static page. Only single-segment paths (/categories) map to templates.
  • Custom PHP route handlers — you cannot register a PHP function to handle a specific URL. Outpost does not have a middleware, hook, or plugin system. All routes render Liquid templates.
  • Route parameters beyond {slug} — patterns like /blog/{year}/{slug} are not supported. Use /blog/{slug} and handle filtering within the template.
Tip — If you need custom server-side logic for a specific route, create a standalone PHP file at your document root (e.g. custom-endpoint.php) and configure your web server to serve it directly. This bypasses Outpost's router entirely.

Dev server vs. production differences

The PHP built-in server used in development (php -S localhost:8000 -t . front-router.php) and a production Apache/Nginx server both route requests through the same PHP logic, but there are subtle differences to be aware of:

Static file handling

On the dev server, the router script is called for every request including static files. The router checks whether the request maps to a real file and returns false to tell PHP to serve it natively. On production Apache/Nginx, the server handles static files before PHP is invoked — PHP never sees those requests.

REQUEST_URI reliability

On the PHP built-in server, $_SERVER['SCRIPT_FILENAME'] may point to the router script itself for all requests, making it unreliable for detecting whether a request is for a real PHP file. Outpost uses $_SERVER['REQUEST_URI'] for path-based decisions instead, which works consistently in both environments.

Apache .htaccess

On Apache, the following .htaccess at the document root routes all non-static requests to index.php:

RewriteEngine On
RewriteCond %{REQUEST_FILENAME} !-f
RewriteCond %{REQUEST_FILENAME} !-d
RewriteRule ^ index.php [L]

Without this rule, direct PHP requests work but pretty URLs like /about return a 404 from Apache before PHP is ever reached.

Nginx configuration

On Nginx, the equivalent configuration in the server block is:

location / {
    try_files $uri $uri/ /index.php?$query_string;
}
Tip — When troubleshooting routing issues on the dev server, check the PHP built-in server output in your terminal. It logs every request and whether it was handled by PHP or served statically. A 404 in the terminal for a file that should exist is usually a path issue in the template.
